title: Creative Imagination and Mystical Experience — v6
author: Henry Corbin

body.text: A cognitive organ with a universe of its own — a universe as real as the physical one. That universe is the subject of this essay.

Here we shall not be dealing with imagination in the usual sense of the word: neither with fantasy, profane or otherwise, nor with the organ which produces imaginings identified with the unreal. We shall be speaking of an absolutely basic function, correlated with a universe peculiar to it, a universe endowed with a perfectly objective existence and perceived precisely through the Imagination. Today, with the help of phenomenology, we are able to examine the way in which man experiences his relationship to the world without reducing the objective data of this experience to data of sense perception or limiting the field of true and meaningful knowledge to the mere operations of the rational understanding.

body.caption: The Imagination has noetic value: it grasps something real.

body.text: The Imagination has noetic value. It grasps something real that lies beyond the reach of both the senses and the intellect. The Sufis call this world alam al-mithal — the world of the Image. An intermediate world of Idea-Images, archetypal figures, subtle substances. The world of theophanic vision.

For them the world is objectively and actually threefold: between the universe that can be apprehended by pure intellectual perception and the universe perceptible to the senses, there is an intermediate world, the world of Idea-Images, of archetypal figures, of subtle substances, of immaterial matter. This world is as real and objective as the intelligible and sensible world. The organ of this universe is the active Imagination; it is the place of theophanic visions, the scene on which visionary events and symbolic histories appear in their true reality.

body.caption: The Annunciation as the paradigm of creative imagination.

body.text: Corbin gives Ibn Arabi's teaching a name the Shaykh never used: creative imagination. The word is Corbin's, but the reality it names is Ibn Arabi's — the human faculty corresponding to divine self-disclosure, the mirror in which theophany takes form. Avicennism identifies it with the Holy Spirit, with the Angel Gabriel. The Annunciation is the paradigm: imagination as the medium through which a new reality enters the world.

We shall try to show in what sense this Imagination is creative: because it is essentially the active Imagination and because its activity defines it essentially as a theophanic Imagination. Avicennism identifies it with the Holy Spirit, that is, with the Angel Gabriel as the Angel of Knowledge and of Revelation. The Annunciation of Gabriel to Mary is the paradigm of imagination as the medium through which a new reality enters the world. Hence the Imagination is the very foundation of prophetic philosophy.

body.caption: Allegory vs symbol: the cipher of a mystery.

body.text: Allegory versus symbol. The distinction runs through everything Corbin wrote because the difference between them is the difference between studying a map and walking the territory. Allegory is rational — it takes what is already known and dresses it in different clothes. Symbol is the only way to say what cannot be said any other way. A symbol demands to be deciphered again and again, like a musical score that calls for ever new performance.

At this point we must recapitulate the distinction, fundamental for us, between allegory and symbol. Allegory is a rational operation, implying no transition either to a new plane of being or to a new depth of consciousness; it is a figuration, at an identical level of consciousness, of what might very well be known in a different way. The symbol announces a plane of consciousness distinct from that of rational evidence; it is the cipher of a mystery, the only means of saying something that cannot be apprehended in any other way. A symbol is never explained once and for all, but must be deciphered over and over again, just as a musical score is never deciphered once and for all, but calls for ever new execution.

body.caption: The two eyes of the heart: reason and imagination.

body.text: Ibn Arabi describes the heart as having two eyes. Reason perceives God's incomparability, His transcendence, the fact that nothing is like Him. Imagination perceives His similarity, His presence in all things. Neither eye sees falsely. Neither sees fully on its own. The complete spiritual life requires both — oscillating between them like the heart's own beating. One beat: incomparability. Next beat: presence. The heart is the metronome.

According to Ibn Arabi, the heart has two eyes, reason and imagination, and the dominance of either distorts perception and awareness. The heart, which in itself is unitary consciousness, must become attuned to its own fluctuation, at one beat seeing God's incomparability with the eye of reason, at the next seeing His similarity with the eye of imagination. These two demarcate the contours of ontology and epistemology. The first alludes to the unifying oneness of Being, perceived by imagination, and the second to the differentiating manyness of knowledge and discernment, perceived by reason. The Real is the One and the Many, One in Essence and many in names.

The Burning Bush. Moses sees a fire that is also more than a fire — the organ that perceives the 'more' is the active Imagination. Without it, the Burning Bush is only a brushwood fire and the Voice is only the wind. The Imagination perceives the additional dimension that is actually there.

The active Imagination guides, anticipates, molds sense perception, that is why it transmutes sensory data into symbols. The Burning Bush is only a brushwood fire if it is merely perceived by the sensory organs. In order that Moses may perceive the Burning Bush and hear the Voice calling him, an organ of trans-sensory perception is needed. The active Imagination is the preeminent mirror, the epiphanic place of the Images of the archetypal world. When directed towards archetypal realities, it brings these realities into harmony and resonance with the world. When directed solely downwards towards matter, it can only produce images which are fantastic, imaginary, unreal.

Ibn Arabi develops a science of the Imagination in his chapter on prayer. Prayer as dialogue — a psalmody in two alternating voices, one human, one divine. The Imagination is the medium through which the divine self-disclosure takes form. The mystic must imagine God as present before him. Through that act of imagining, the presence becomes real.

Ibn Arabi outlines a science of the Imagination and provides a schema of the themes involved. The organ of Prayer is the heart, the psycho-spiritual organ, with its concentration of energy, its himma. The role of prayer is shared between God and man, because Creation like theophany is shared between Him who shows Himself and him to whom it is shown. We witness and participate in an entire ceremonial of meditation, a psalmody in two alternating voices, one human the other divine. This psalmody perpetually reconstitutes, recreates the solidarity and interdependence of the Creator and His creature.

Three stages. First: the faithful places himself in the presence of God and converses with Him. Second: he imagines God as present, facing him. Third: he attains intuitive vision, contemplating God in the subtle center of the heart. Conversation. Imagination. Vision. That progression is the path Ibn Arabi maps. It is not a ladder you climb once. It is a cycle you repeat.

Ibn Arabi distinguishes three successive moments in prayer. First, the faithful must place himself in the company of his God and converse with Him. In an intermediate moment the faithful must imagine his God as present in his Qibla, that is, facing him. Finally, in a third moment, the faithful must attain to intuitive vision or visualization, contemplating his God in the subtle center which is the heart, and simultaneously hear the divine voice vibrating in all manifest things.

Microcosmic angelology. Just as the macrocosm has its angels, the human body has its own angelic faculties — physical, psychic, and spiritual powers that, when transformed by the imaginative discipline, become the Angels of the microcosm. The mystic becomes the Imam of this angelic court within himself. The Godhead is in mankind as an Image is in a mirror. The place of this Presence is the theophanic Imagination.

Since each part of the cosmology has its homologue in man, the whole universe is in him. And just as the Angels of the macrocosm sprang from the faculties of the Primordial Man, from the Angel called Spirit, so the Angels of the microcosm are the physical, psychic, and spiritual faculties of the individual man. Represented as Angels, these faculties are transformed into subtle centers and organs. The Godhead is in mankind as an Image is in a mirror. The place of this Presence is the consciousness of the individual believer, or more exactly, the theophanic Imagination invested in him.

body.caption: The divine Alter Ego: the mystic's transcendent self.

body.text: The journey culminates in an encounter with a being who is both the mystic and more than the mystic. The divine Alter Ego. Ibn Arabi's visionary dialogue with this being is the template for all spiritual practice. The circumambulation of the heart — which is the Temple. The recognition that the mystery of the Divine Essence is nothing other than the Temple of the heart. You do not encounter God directly. You enter the heart, and there He is.

The being who is the mystic's transcendent self, his divine Alter Ego, reveals himself, and the mystic does not hesitate to recognize him. The mystery of the Divine Essence is no other than the Temple of the heart, and it is around the heart that the spiritual pilgrim circumambulates. One does not encounter the Divine Essence directly; for it is itself the Temple, the Mystery of the heart, into which the mystic penetrates when, having achieved the microcosmic plenitude of the Perfect Man, he encounters the Form of God which is that of His Angels, that is to say, the theophany constitutive of his being.

The Temple is the scene of theophany. The heart is where the dialogue between Lover and Beloved is enacted. That dialogue is what Ibn Arabi means by prayer. The Imagination is the organ through which the divine reveals itself to human consciousness, and through which human consciousness participates in the divine reality. The cosmos requires a middle, and the middle requires an organ — the one we have been trained to distrust our whole lives.

Never can the manifest, visible things be the causes of other visible things; an immaterial cause is required. The Temple is the scene of theophany, the heart where the dialogue between Lover and Beloved is enacted, and that is why this dialogue is the Prayer of God. The Godhead is in mankind as an Image is in a mirror. The place of this Presence is the consciousness of the individual believer, the theophanic Imagination invested in him.
